neighborhoods.name: Pennsylvania Avenue Corridor / West Baltimore
primary_groups: African Americans from Virginia, the Carolinas, Maryland's Eastern Shore
neighborhoods.character: Baltimore's primary Black commercial and cultural district along Pennsylvania Avenue from Dolphin Street to North Avenue. Unlike many Northern cities, Baltimore had a large, established Black population before the Great Migration. The Avenue was lined with Black-owned businesses, theaters (the Royal Theatre hosted every major Black entertainer), and professional offices. Row houses in West Baltimore were the residential anchor.
key_institutions: Sharp Street Memorial United Methodist Church
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} arrived in Baltimore, Pennsylvania Avenue was already the center of Black life — the Afro-American newspaper had been publishing since 1892, and the Avenue's theaters, churches, and barbershops were a functioning city within a city.

neighborhoods.name: Fells Point / Canton
neighborhoods.character: Baltimore's waterfront immigrant neighborhood, centered on the docks and shipyards along the Patapsco River. Fells Point was the primary point of disembarkation for immigrants arriving at the Port of Baltimore. German, Polish, and Irish immigrants settled in tight rows of brick houses within walking distance of the waterfront. Canton, to the east, developed as a Polish and Ukrainian working-class neighborhood.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} arrived at the Port of Baltimore, Fells Point was probably the first neighborhood they saw — the brick row houses climbing the hill from the waterfront were home to generation after generation of immigrant families.

neighborhoods.name: Highlandtown / Greektown
neighborhoods.character: Working-class neighborhood east of the harbor. Highlandtown's Eastern Avenue was the commercial spine, with ethnic bakeries, butcher shops, and taverns. The Greek community concentrated along Eastern Avenue near Ponca Street — one of the largest Greek communities in the United States. Row houses were the universal housing type.
key_institutions: Czech and Slovak social halls on Eastern Avenue
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: Highlandtown was where Eastern European and Mediterranean immigrants built lives within walking distance of the factories — if {ancestor_name} was Greek, Czech, or Polish, Eastern Avenue's shops and churches were the center of daily life.

major_employers.name: Bethlehem Steel — Sparrows Point
major_employers.wages: Common laborers: $2-3/day (1910s). Skilled steelworkers: $4-6/day. By mid-century, union contracts brought $2-4/hr with benefits.
major_employers.working_conditions: The Sparrows Point steel mill, southeast of Baltimore on the Chesapeake Bay, was the largest tidewater steel mill in the world. At its peak, it employed over 30,000 workers. The company built the adjacent town of Sparrows Point with company housing organized by race — white workers in one section, Black workers in a separate, inferior section. Working conditions matched other steel mills: extreme heat, dangerous machinery, long shifts.
major_employers.narrative_hook: Sparrows Point was a world of its own — if {ancestor_name} worked Bethlehem Steel, the mill on the edge of the Chesapeake employed more people than most towns and the company owned the houses, the stores, and the hospital.

major_employers.name: Baltimore & Ohio Railroad
major_employers.wages: Varied by position. Section hands: $1.25-2.00/day. Skilled mechanics at the Mount Clare Shops: $2.50-4.00/day. The B&O was Baltimore's signature company.
major_employers.working_conditions: The B&O was the first commercial railroad in the United States, and its Mount Clare Shops in West Baltimore were the largest railroad repair facility in the country. The railroad employed workers from every ethnic community. Irish immigrants built much of the original trackage. Black workers served as porters, firemen, and laborers. The 1877 Great Railroad Strike began on the B&O in Martinsburg, West Virginia, and spread to Baltimore with deadly violence.
major_employers.narrative_hook: The B&O Railroad built Baltimore and employed it — if {ancestor_name} worked for the railroad, the Mount Clare Shops were probably the workplace, and the payroll records of America's first railroad may document the employment.

major_employers.name: Canning Industry / Oyster Packing
major_employers.wages: Piecework: oyster shuckers earned $0.05-0.10 per gallon of shucked oysters. Cannery workers: $0.50-1.00/day. Low-wage, seasonal work.
major_employers.working_conditions: Baltimore was the canning capital of America. Oyster packing and vegetable canning employed thousands of workers — predominantly Black women and immigrant women. The work was seasonal, low-paid, and physically demanding. Shucking oysters required skill but offered no job security. Children worked alongside adults in many canneries.
major_employers.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} was a woman arriving in Baltimore with no trade, the canneries along the harbor offered immediate employment — the pay was poor and the work was seasonal, but oyster shucking and vegetable canning were open to anyone with fast hands.

port_of_entry_details.facility: Locust Point Immigration Pier (1868-1914). Baltimore was the second-busiest immigration port on the East Coast after New York. The North German Lloyd and Hamburg America steamship lines ran direct service from Bremen and Hamburg to Locust Point. Many immigrants bound for the Midwest disembarked at Locust Point and immediately boarded B&O Railroad trains.
port_of_entry_details.processing: Locust Point processed immigrants more quickly and less formally than Ellis Island. The steamship companies handled much of the processing. The B&O Railroad depot was adjacent to the pier, allowing direct transfer. German, Czech, Polish, and Lithuanian immigrants arrived in especially large numbers.
records_generated: Ship passenger manifests (National Archives, available through Ancestry.com and FamilySearch)
Baltimore passenger lists (separate from New York)

housing_patterns.initial_settlement: Baltimore's brick row houses were the universal housing type — narrow, two- or three-story attached homes with marble stoops (steps). Boarding houses near the waterfront or industrial areas served single men. Families rented row houses in ethnic neighborhoods.
housing_patterns.ethnic_clustering: Strong ethnic clustering. Germans in Fells Point and Highlandtown. Poles in Canton and Highlandtown. Greeks along Eastern Avenue. Black residents in West Baltimore along Pennsylvania Avenue and in East Baltimore. Czech and Lithuanian communities in Southeast Baltimore.
housing_patterns.trajectory: Boarding house (months) to rental row house (2-5 years) to owned row house (5-15 years). Baltimore's affordable row house stock made homeownership accessible — the marble stoop was both a social institution and a mark of ownership. The typical trajectory was faster than in more expensive cities.

anti_immigrant_reception.description: Baltimore's position on the border between North and South gave it a complex racial character. The city had a large free Black population before the Civil War but also enforced rigid racial segregation. Anti-immigrant sentiment targeted successive waves of European arrivals.
specific_incidents: 1812 Baltimore Riot — anti-Federalist mob attacked newspaper editors
Know-Nothing violence in the 1850s — Baltimore was a Know-Nothing stronghold, earning the nickname 'Mob Town'
1910 residential segregation ordinance — Baltimore passed the first racial zoning law in the United States
nativist_organizations: Various white neighborhood improvement associations enforcing racial boundaries

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Sharp Street Memorial United Methodist Church
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: African American community — one of the oldest Black churches in America
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Membership rolls, baptism and marriage records dating to the late 18th century. One of the most important Black church archives in the mid-Atlantic region.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: Sharp Street Church has records going back to 1787 — if the {surname} family was part of Baltimore's Black community, this church may hold family records spanning two centuries.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: St. Stanislaus Kostka Church (Baltimore)
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: Polish immigrants in Fells Point and Canton
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Baptism, marriage, and death registers in Polish and Latin. Records held by the Archdiocese of Baltimore.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: If the {surname} family was Polish and settled in Baltimore's waterfront neighborhoods, St. Stanislaus Kostka's sacramental registers probably recorded their family events — the parish was the center of Polish community life in Fells Point.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Greek Orthodox Cathedral of the Annunciation
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: Greek immigrants in Highlandtown and Greektown
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Baptism, marriage, and death registers in Greek and English.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} was Greek and lived in Baltimore, the Cathedral of the Annunciation likely holds family sacramental records — the Greek community in Highlandtown was tight-knit and the church recorded nearly every family milestone.

source_references.author: Seth Rockman
source_references.title: Scraping By: Wage Labor, Slavery, and Survival in Early Baltimore
source_references.notes: Documents working-class life in Baltimore where free Black workers, enslaved people, and white immigrants competed for the same jobs, using almshouse records, court records, and employment data.

source_references.author: Andor Skotnes
source_references.title: A New Deal for All? Race and Class Struggles in Depression-Era Baltimore
source_references.notes: Examines the intersection of race, labor, and politics in Depression-era Baltimore, covering the Black community, immigrant workers, and New Deal programs.

source_references.author: Karen Olson
source_references.title: Wives of Steel: Voices of Women from the Sparrows Point Steelmaking Communities
source_references.notes: Oral history-based study of families in the Sparrows Point company town, documenting the daily lives of steelworker families, including racial segregation in company housing.
